name: Hand Axe
desc: A Hand Axe is a simple one-handed weapon. It

name: Battle Axe
desc: A Battle Axe is a one-handed weapon larger than
a simple hand axe, specialized for combat.

name: Great Axe
desc: A Great Axe is a large, two-handed weapon. It
does a significant amount of damage, and can normally
only be wielded by Medium or Large creatures.
EXECEPTION: Dwarves can wield Great Axes, but no other

name: Pickaxe (Military Pick)
desc: Pick Axe and Military Picks are similar
to axes, but trade the heavy blade for a
sharpened spike. It is not unusual to find
both a pick and blade on the same weapon.

name: Mattock (Footman's Pick)
desc: axes, but trade the heavy blade for a
sharpened spike. It is not unusual to find
both a pick and blade on the same weapon.

name: Dagger
desc: A Dagger is a versatile weapon used in close-
quarters combat and short ranged missle combat. They
are light, easy to conceal and can be thrown with
They are popular with Thieves for swift and silent
kills, with Fighters as a last resort, and with Magic

name: Defending Dagger
desc: features to help catch or defend against
other blades such as an extended or
guard, or forks or tines. When used
weapon, see Combat Options), this weapon
is treated as a Buckler against swords
and daggers only. Defending daggers will
rarely be balanced for throwing. The

name: Shortsword / Cutlass
desc: curved or straight – designed for hacking

name: Longsword / Scimitar
desc: be wielded with one hand by
however they are curved to a

name: Two-Handed Sword
desc: While their size can be un

name: Light Mace
desc: lighter version of a regular mace,

name: Mace
desc: iron or steel on the end of a

name: Mornigstar
desc: mounts spikes or blades on the head.
Whether or not a morningstar may be
used by Clerics in a setting is up

name: Maul / Great Mace
desc: are essentially the Hammer's and Mace's
the Maul or Great Mace become virtually

name: Spear
desc: A Spear is an interstingly versatile weapon. It
can be wielded in melee combat either one- or two-handed,
doing 1d6 or 1d8 damage respectivly, or it can be thrown,
This gives a melee fighter a choice between using a spear
with one hand, allowing them to use a shield for greater
defense and lesser damage or two-handed, for lesser

name: Fork / Trident
desc: a double or triple-pronged head. This head makes it
possible to catch or hold opponents at a distance.
To entangle an opponent is a called attack with a -2
attack penalty and damage is counted as subduing.
These weapons are interstingly versatile. They
can be wielded in melee combat either one- or two-handed,
doing 1d6 or 1d8 damage respectivly, or it can be thrown,
This gives a melee fighter a choice between using a spear
with one hand, allowing them to use a shield for greater
defense and lesser damage or two-handed, for lesser

name: Boar Spear
desc: A Boar Spear adds a crossbar partway back
from the head, not unlike the crossguard of a
sword or dagger. This is meant to prevent an impaled
opponent (such as a boar) from running up the spear
and striking the wielder. It is an interstingly
versatile weapon. It can be wielded in melee combat
either one- or two-handed, doing 1d6 or 1d8 damage
respectivly, or it can be thrown, again doing 1d6 damage.
This gives a melee fighter a choice between using a spear
with one hand, allowing them to use a shield for greater
defense and lesser damage or two-handed, for lesser

name: Lance
desc: spear, and not suited for throwing. A
properly balanced lance may be used one-

name: Pole Arm
desc: ing the ability to hit oppon
in front of the wielder, and to strike first against

name: Chain (min weight & price)
desc: A Chain is the simplest form of Chain Weapon
– a length of chain, swung as a weapon.

name: Flail
desc: Flails extend the concept of a chain by
adding a weighted striking head of some kind,
from long heads mounted on a short cord or
hinge, to a variety of ball and chain weapons.
This includes weapons like nunchaku. Most flails
are usable by Clerics, though spiked flails
(similar to a morningstar) may be prohibited.

name: Great Flail
desc: A Great Flail takes the concept of a regular
flail to an extreme. Like a great sword, these
flails are unwieldy at best, consisting usually of
a massive spiked ball on the end of a chain, which
in turn is mounted to the end of a stout handle.
These weapons deliver an enormous amount of damage
at the expense of speed. Due to the spikes, it is
up to the GM to decide if Clerics may wield Great

name: Whip
desc: The Whip operates on a similar principle to the
chain, with an even greater emphasis on speed – to the
point that it acts more as a slashing weapon. Whips do
poorly against hard armors or thick hides, and will
not do damage to targets with an armor or natural AC of

name: Club/Cudgel/Walking Staff
desc: These weapons are lumped together because they are:
They require very little in the way of training and are
thus quite popular among peasants and Magic Users.

name: Silver Walking Stick/Staff
desc: These weapons are lumped together because they are:
They require very little in the way of training and are
thus quite popular among peasants and Magic Users.
These items have been coated with silver, allowing them to
damage lycanthropes and other such creatures.

name: Greatclub
desc: A <>Greatclub is just a larger, heavier, more damaging
club. It often has knots or even jagged stones embedded
in it for greater damage and a menacing look.

name: Sap / Blackjack
desc: The Sap or Blackjack is a leather pouch
weighted with sand or lead shot. This weapon is intended
to inflict subduing damage only. When used in a sneak
attack (as by Thieves), the blackjack has a chance of
knocking its victim unconscious with a blow to the head.

name: Hook
desc: Hooks cover a variety of hook-shaped weapons or
implements, from short-handled meat hooks to gaff hooks
and shepherd's crooks (the latter being Medium-sized). A
hook can have a sharpened point, or simply be used as an

name: Sickle
desc: Sickles cover a variety of inward-curved cutting
weapons, derived from one-handed grain harvesting
tools. The statistics here are for sickles specifically
forged & balanced for use as weapons. The actual farm

name: Scythe
desc: Scythes cover a variety of inward-curved cutting
weapons, derived from two-handed grain harvesting
tools. The statistics here are for scythes specifically
forged & balanced for use as weapons. The actual farm

name: Crowbar
desc: Improvised Weapons are simply those weapons not
actually designed for fighting, like a random bottle
or chair grabbed in a brawl, a pair of scissors used
in self-defense, a peasant's hastily grabbed farm tool
These weapons are usually not as sturdy or well-balanced
as actual weapons. Improvised weapons suffer a -2 to
strike in melee and poor range and accuracy when thrown.

name: Frying Pan
desc: Improvised Weapons are simply those weapons not
actually designed for fighting, like a random bottle
or chair grabbed in a brawl, a pair of scissors used
in self-defense, a peasant's hastily grabbed farm tool
These weapons are usually not as sturdy or well-balanced
as actual weapons. Improvised weapons suffer a -2 to
strike in melee and poor range and accuracy when thrown.

name: Pitchfork
desc: Improvised Weapons are simply those weapons not
actually designed for fighting, like a random bottle
or chair grabbed in a brawl, a pair of scissors used
in self-defense, a peasant's hastily grabbed farm tool
These weapons are usually not as sturdy or well-balanced
as actual weapons. Improvised weapons suffer a -2 to
strike in melee and poor range and accuracy when thrown.

name: Spade
desc: Improvised Weapons are simply those weapons not
actually designed for fighting, like a random bottle
or chair grabbed in a brawl, a pair of scissors used
in self-defense, a peasant's hastily grabbed farm tool
These weapons are usually not as sturdy or well-balanced
as actual weapons. Improvised weapons suffer a -2 to
strike in melee and poor range and accuracy when thrown.

name: Spiked Gauntlet / Cestus
desc: armored 'gloves' with added weight, ridges, studs, etc.
to give a punch a little extra impact. These simply
A character cannot deliver subduing damage while wearing these.

name: Shortbow
desc: A > is a simple bow that can be fired
from horseback by Medium sized or larger creatures.

name: Shortbow Arrow
desc: Shortbow arrows are designed to be fired from a

name: Silver Shortbow Arrow
desc: The heads of Silver Shortbow Arrows have been
coated with silver, allowing them to damage lycanthropes

name: Longbow
desc: A Longbow is larger and has more range than a
Shortbow. It typically requires the user to be standing
while firing. It cannot be wielded by creature smaller

name: Longbow Arrow
desc: Longbow Arrows are designed to be fired from a

name: Silver Longbow Arrow
desc: The heads of Silver Longbow Arrows have been
coated with silver, allowing them to damage lycanthropes

name: Light Crossbow
desc: Light Crossbows are simple, handheld devices that
use a mechanical assembly to fire bolts. They are easy to
wield and require less strength to operate compared to
Light crossbows offer superior accuracy and power over
short to mid-range distances. They are favored by
adventurers who value reliability and ease of use.

name: Light Quarrel
desc: Light Quarrels are bolts designed to be fired
from a Light Crossbow. 10 of them weigh 1 pound.

name: Silver Light Quarrel
desc: The heads of these <b>Silver Light Quarrels have been
coated with silver, allowing them to damage lycanthropes

name: Heavy Crossbow
desc: Heavy Crossbows are more advanced versions of
Light Crossbows, featuring a larger frame and increased
Heavy Crossbows require more strength to handle
effectively but they have greater piercing power and
Heavy Crossbows are ideal for adventurers who want to
deliver devastating shots to their enemies.

name: Heavy Quarrel
desc: Heavy Quarrels are bolts designed to be fired
from a Light Crossbow. 10 of them weigh 1 pound.

name: Silver Heavy Quarrel
desc: The heads of these Silver Heavy Quarrels have
been coated with silver, allowing them to damage

name: Hand Crossbow
desc: Hand Crossbows are something of a novelty
– rare, fairly delicate and difficult to craft. They
are easy to draw and set (firing once per round), but
have short range and less penetration (damage) compared
Their small size and ease of use make these popular for
assassination using poisoned bolts. As the name
suggests, these can be wielded with one hand. The short
bolts require a special-sized bolt case.

name: Hand Quarrel
desc: <b>Hand Quarrels<b> are crossbow bolts specially designed
to fit in the tiny groove of a Hand Crossbow. The heads
are often coated with poison due to their minimal damage
Ten of these bolts weigh one pound.

name: Silver Hand Quarrel
desc: <b>Hand Quarrels<b> are crossbow bolts specially designed
to fit in the tiny groove of a Hand Crossbow. The heads
are often coated with poison due to their minimal damage
Ten of these bolts weigh one pound.
The heads of these bolts have been coated with silver,
allowing them to damage lycanthropes and other such

name: Bullet Crossbow
desc: The Bullet Crossbow is a version of the
light crossbow designed to fire small pellets
(sling bullets) rather than bolts. At the GM's
discretion, some Clerics may be permitted to

name: Sling
desc: A Sling is a projectile weapon typically used
to hand-throw a blunt projectile such as a stone or lead
A sling has a small pouch in the middle of two cords,
where a projectile is placed. There is a loop on the end
of one of the cords and a tab on the end of the other.
Depending on the design, either the middle finger or the
wrist is placed through the loop and the tab is placed
between the thumb and forefinger. The sling is swung in
an arc, and the tab released at a precise moment,
causing the projectile to fly towards the target.

name: Sling Bullet
desc: Sling Bullets are small metal balls made of lead
or iron, sized to fit within the pouch of a sling.

name: Sling Stone
desc: Sling Stones are small round stones, sized to fit
within the pouch of a sling.

name: Bola (E)
desc: A Bola is made from 2-3 weights attached
by lengths of cord or leather. It is a hunting
weapon designed primarily to snare targets.

name: Dart / Throwing Blade
desc: similar in size and design to a blowgun dart
Throwing blades are light, edged or pointed
throwing weapons. This includes throwing knives,

name: Javelin
desc: spear with better range but inflicting less
damage. These include the Roman pilum.

name: Blowgun
desc: metal or wood, which use the wielders' own
lung power to fire small darts (or potentially
other projectiles). These weapons are typically
around 4 feet in length, but lengths of up to 7
Traditionally a hunting weapon, a blowgun will
occasionally find use in combat, and are

name: Blowgun Dart
desc: projectiles designed to be fired from a

name: Net (E)
desc: Nets are only used to snare opponents. A net
for combat use is typically about 10 feet in
diameter, with a series of weights around the
edges to help spread the net in flight, and
hold down the edges to better trap targets.
Nets can be used in melee, but only to attempt
to tangle a weapon. It must be thrown to

name: Padded or Quilted
desc: Padded Armor is created from layers of
cloth placed on top of each other. It is often
ceremonial rather than functional, but does
offer minimal amounts of protection. The
layers of cloth are great for winter months
because they provide solid insulation, but will
get very sweaty and dirty unless properly
laundered often. Several types of metal armors
include a layer of padded armor or ‘arming

name: Hide
desc: Hide Armor is much like leather in its working
and protection. Often hide armor can be found
in remote areas or barbaric lands; it is the
most common type used by orcs and many other
humanoids. The process of creating hide armor
involves curing the beast's hide and sewing it
to clothing. It is often hot and itchy, but
offers protection similar to regular leather

name: Leather
desc: leather.The breastplate and shoulder sections consist of
stiffer, thicker pieces that have been boiled in oil. The
rest of the armor is made of softer pieces that allow
This armor is especially prized by thieves, since it
provides defense and allows silent movement.

name: Studded Leather
desc: regular leather armor, but instead of harden
ing the leather, metal studs and/or small
plates are riveted to it. The studs coupled
with the leather material provide added prot
ection from attacks at the slight cost of

name: Ring Mail
desc: Ring Mail Armor consists of rings of
steel directly sewn to an undergarment. The
rings themselves offer great flexibility and
decent protection. The underlying garment is
usually soft and supple leather which can

name: Brigandine
desc: Brigandine Armor consists of a cloth or
leather garment, lined with small oblong steel
plates set inside or between layers, and riveted
to the fabric. Designs may include sleeves or be
worn with vambraces. The rivets or nails attach
ing the plates to the fabric are visible on the
outside and often decorated. Brigandine is
typically worn over a padded undergarment; if
provides an Armor Class of 14 at a weight of 20
pounds. This type of armor would also include
armors with small plates attached to the outside
of a quilted or padded garment.

name: Chain Mail
desc: Chain Mail is constructed from hundreds, if not
thousands of interlocking metal rings.  The most common
type is a shirt of mail worn over a padded garment known
as a gambeson. Chain mail commonly has an attached hood
of rings to protect the head and neck.

name: Scale Mail
desc: Scale Mail Armor is made of metal scales
directly woven to an undergarment that offers
flexibility and moderate protection. A related
armor type is Lamellar, where the individual
scales or plates are connected together, rather
than to a cloth or leather backing.

name: Splint Mail
desc: Splint Mail Armor is a form of chain
mail where small strips of thick metal are
interwoven amongst the rings of the mail. These
small embedded plates offer excellent coverage
and better protection than regular chain mail at
the price of additional weight and less
flexibility. This also covers other types of
cuirass, or Mail and Plate armors without a full

name: Banded Mail
desc: Banded Mail Armor is a type of laminar
armor, with overlapping strips or bands of
metal. The Roman lorica segmentata is an
example of the form. The armor will also
sleeves. This type of armor typically does not
include an integrated backing or chain coat (or
a very light one), and is significantly lighter
than other armors in the same category. Some
versions may easily collapse for storage. The
number of connecting parts, and the fittings
needed to properly fit and secure this armor,
may make banded armors more difficult to

name: Plate Mail
desc: The ultimate in armored protection, Plate Mail is
made from plates of metal that are fitted over arms,
legs and torso, supplemented with portions of chain mail.
Plate armor includes gaunlets and a helmet.
Plate mail must be custom-fitted to the wearer. Although,
if a new owner is not too differently sized, it can
usually be adjusted by an armorsmith.

name: Field Plate
desc: pieces of plate armor, fastened together with a
variety of complicated sliding parts, hinges,
and straps. Small pieces of chain mail are used
to cover hard-to-protect joints, such as behind
the knees and under the arms. Field plate armor
provides greater protection than regular plate
mail with a relatively small increase in
weight, but must be custom fitted to the
wearer; this generally means that a character
purchasing this sort of armor must wait at least
a full week between placing the order and
receiving the finished suit. Field plate armor
is worn over a padded undergarment, and if worn
without the undergarment, the Armor Class prov
ided is 17 at a weight of 60 pounds.

name: Full Plate
desc: ull Plate Armor is generally identical to
field plate armor, but is of superior design and
manufacture; only an expert armorer can fashion
it. The plates cover the wearer more fully, and
chain mail is generally not used at all. Also,
the plates themselves are designed to deflect
weapon strikes away from any otherwise-vulner
able joint in the armor. Those who wear Full
plate suffer a -1 penalty to attack rolls due
to limited mobility while wearing the armor.
Full plate cannot be put on alone and takes at
least 1 turn to get into with assistance.

name: Buckler
desc: A Buckler is a small shield worn on the
forearm or held by a single handle to block
incoming attacks. It is usually about a foot
in diameter. The wearer of a buckler receives
+1 to their Armor Class when in melee combat,
but gains no protection against missile fire.

name: Medium Shield
desc: A Medium Shield is what most people
think of when they say 'Shield'. It is a
defensive device held in a melee fighter's
off hand. It is the 'board' part of the
fighting style known as 'Sword and Board'.
However, a shield can be employed with any
Shields are commonly made of split-resistant
wood such as poplar and are often reinforced
with metal, covered with leather or both.
Shields made entirely of metal exist, but are

name: Tower Shield
desc: A Tower Shield is a very large shield
that is almost the height of the wearer; such
a shield is generally rectangular, but oval,
elliptical, and curved shapes are not uncommon.
Because of the bulky and cumbersome nature of
such a large shield, it only offers protection
similar to a medium shield while in melee (+1)
yet gives superior protection against missile

name: BARDING
desc: Barding is armor designed to be worn by

name: Small Mount (S rider)
desc: Dwarves and Halflings. This includes ponies,
riding dogs, and war rams. This is also suitable
for other dogs (guard dogs, for example) over

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Plate Barding
desc: pieces or segmented metal coat with chain mail
support in other areas. The overall look is
somewhere between plate mail and field plate.

name: Dog (Non-mount)
desc: animals including hunting cats and the smaller
of guard or hunting dogs. Most animals of this
size will not tolerate armor heavier than mail,

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Regular Mount (M rider)
desc: may, at the DM's discretion, cover other horse-
sized animals commonly used in an area/society/
world, such as camels, giant lizards or even

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Plate Barding
desc: pieces or segmented metal coat with chain mail
support in other areas. The overall look is
somewhere between plate mail and field plate.

name: Large Mount
desc: significantly larger than a draft horse, such
as an elephant. For the truly titanic, apply

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Plate Barding
desc: pieces or segmented metal coat with chain mail
support in other areas. The overall look is
somewhere between plate mail and field plate.

name: Flying Mount
desc: Flying Mount costs are applied on top
of the base size cost. This is in part because
of the relative rarity of flying mounts, as
well as the more complex designs needed to
mounts cannot fly in armor heavier than light

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Plate Barding
desc: pieces or segmented metal coat with chain mail
support in other areas. The overall look is
somewhere between plate mail and field plate.

name: Exotic Mount
desc: to a culture or game setting. Generally this
is for exotic creatures, but may apply to
'normal' mounts outside of their usual setting
(armor for a camel or elephant; new barding for
a war ram outside of Dwarven lands, etc.).
Listed costs are at minimum multiplier and

name: Light Barding
desc: leather, with reinforced leather or metal plates
for added protection in key areas. Generally
the effect is similar to studded leather.

name: Mail Barding
desc: Mail Barding is a coat of chain or scale
armor with cured leather or metal guards for
head, chest, and other strategic locations. In
many ways, the effect is similar to splint or

name: Plate Barding
desc: pieces or segmented metal coat with chain mail
support in other areas. The overall look is
somewhere between plate mail and field plate.

name: Cooking Supplies (per week)
desc: needed for cooking on the road, including

name: Fire Grate
desc: A Fire Grate is a simple iron framework that
is placed over a fire, providing a level surface to

name: Fishhook
desc: Fishhooks are used mostly for catching fish,
but can also be used to fasten things together.

name: Iron Pan (Frying)
desc: An Iron Pan, also called a frypan or skillet,
is a flat-bottomed cookware used for frying, searing,
and browning foods. It typically ranges from 8 to
12 inches in diameter with relatively low sides that
flare outwards, a long handle, and no lid.

name: Iron Pot
desc: An Iron Pot is a cooking vessel that is
usually round and deep, often with a handle and lid.
It is primarily used for cooking soups and stews.

name: Dry, 1 week
desc: Dry Rations may consist of dry bread, hard
cheese, dried fruit, nuts, beans, jerky, or any other
food which will not 'go bad' in less than about a
month (if not longer). Dry rations are generally sold
in quantities sufficient for one character for a week,
and are packaged in waxed or oiled cloth to protect them.

name: Elven Waybread, 1 week
desc: Elven Waybread is famously described to fill a
man's stomach for a day with just one bite. While not
quite this sustaining, this dry but not-bad-tasting
tack biscuit is very nourishing. A single small cake

name: Standard (perishable), 1 day
desc: fruits and vegetables, and fresh breads or biscuits,
often wrapped in cloth. These are tastier than dry
rations, but will generally only keep for a few days in
all but the coldest or driest climates (up to 1 week).

name: Tea Pot
desc: A Tea Pot is a vessel with a handle, pouring
spout and lid.  Tea leaves are placed in the pot,
which is then filled with water that has been heated
in a separate pot. Once the tea leaves have steeped
for long enough (varies according to taste), The tea
is poured out into cups or mugs.

name: Tripod, cooking
desc: A Cooking Tripod is a collapsible device of
3 metal poles (about 4 feet long) attached at one end.
The poles are placed over a fire so that a cooking
pot can be suspended over it.

name: Holy Symbol
desc: Holy Symbols come in as many varieites as
there are religions. While they all have
as well. While anyone can own a holy symbol
about them. In the hands of a true cleric,
however, they become the focus for their
spells and the primary tool for turning or

name: Holy Symbol, Ornate
desc: ceremonially rather than while adventuring. Aside
from being an indicaor of status, they offer no

name: Holy Water, per vial
desc: Holy Water is harmful to undead creatures. A
character may throw a flask of holy water as a
grenade-like missile, which will break and release the
contents if thrown against the body of a corporeal
creature. To use it against an incorporeal creature it
must be opened and poured or splashed onto the target,
generally requiring the attacker to be directly adjacent
Each flask of holy water can inflict 1d8 points of
damage to such a monster. In addition, each additional
undead monster within 5 feet of the point of impact
receives 1d6 points of damage from the splash. Holy
Water is only effective for one round.

name: Quiver or Bolt case (20)
desc: A Quiver is an open container used to hold
arrows. A Bolt Case is a similar sort of container
for crossbow bolts. In either case, the standard
capacity is 20 missiles. The length of a quiver or
bolt case must match the length of the ammunition
for it to be useful; therefore, there are longbow
and shortbow quivers and light and heavy crossbow
bolt cases. The price is the same regardless of the

name: Thieves' picks and tools
desc: Thieves' Picks and Tools are required for the use
of Thief abilities such as opening locks and removing
traps. These abilities may not be usable without
appropriate tools, or may be used at a penalty at the

name: Whetstone
desc: A Whetstone is used to sharpen and maintain
edged weapons such as swords, daggers, and axes.

name: Bed Roll
desc: A Bed Roll is a portable, rolled-up
arrangement consisting of a blanket and a
mattress pad, often wrapped in a waterproof
canvas shell. Designed for portability and
outdoor use, it acts as a self-contained bed,
often featuring straps for easy transport.

name: Beeswax
desc: Beeswax is a soft wax, useful for

name: Candles, 12
desc: A Candle will shed light over a 5
foot radius, with dim light extending a
further 5 feet. A normal candle will burn
about 3 turns per inch of height.

name: Chalk, colored, small bag of pieces
desc: through a dungeon or ruin to ensure that the
adventurers can find their way back out again.

name: Chalk, small bag of pieces
desc: through a dungeon or ruin to ensure that the
adventurers can find their way back out again.

name: Charcoal stic
desc: carbonized wood, which can be used like chalk
(making black markings), or may be added to

name: Coal Keeper
desc: A Coal Keeper is a small, lined
ceramic pot designed to hold and keep a
small coal or ember lit for several hours.
While this will resist moisture and wind,

name: Cord/ Strap, per 3 ft
desc: length of thin rope, leather, or a short
belt for tying something to an arm, leg, or
other accessory. This can secure a scabbard,
attach a pouch, or fasten a bed roll to a

name: Flask, Silver
desc: stoppers that hold between 8 and 12 oz of
liquid. While it may tarnish with time, a

name: Flask, Steel
desc: stoppers that hold between 8 and 12 oz of
liquid. Care should be taken to avoid rust.

name: Grappling Hook
desc: metal hook attached to a rope, designed to
be thrown, or dropped, to catch onto
objects, ledges, or rigging. Used for
feature 3-4 claws and provide a sturdy anchor

name: Hammock
desc: A Hammock is a swinging bed or couch
made of canvas or netted cord, suspended by
lines between two supports (such as trees
or poles). It is designed to keep a person

name: Ladder, Rope, 25ft
desc: A Rope Ladder is a regularly knotted
rope, or two lengths of rope, strung with
rungs, with a single line at top for a
hook. It typically has an 850 lb capacity.

name: Lantern, Bullseye
desc: A Bullseye Lantern is similar to a
hooded lantern, only it is closed on all
but one lensed side. This lamp projects
light up to 30 feet away, and 30 feet at
its widest, and includes a shutter. It will
consume a flask of oil in 18+1d6 turns.

name: Lantern, Hooded
desc: mechanism to close off the light and
prevent it from being seen. A Lantern will
provide light covering a 30 foot radius;
dim light will extend about 20 feet
further. A lantern will consume a flask of

name: Lens, small
desc: A Lens enlarges the image of an
object. A bare lens is smaller than a
magnifying glass and is limited to 2-3x
magnification. It can be used to attempt
to start fires using strong sunlight.

name: Lock, Excellent
desc: A Lock is a mechanical device used
to secure something. They can be built into
an object, such as a door or chest. It can
also exist separately as a padlock.
Excellent locks are intricate and highly
complex. They should be harder to pick
than normal locks. A suggested penalty is
10% for each 100gp spent in the lock's

name: Lock, Good
desc: A Lock is a mechanical device used
to secure something. They can be built into
an object, such as a door or chest. It can
also exist separately as a padlock.

name: Lock, Poor
desc: A Lock is a mechanical device used
to secure something. They can be built into
an object, such as a door or chest. It can
also exist separately as a padlock.
Poor locks are cheaply made and fairly
simple in construction. They should be easier
to pick than normal locks. A suggested bonus
is 10% for each gp less than 21gp spent in

name: Magnet, Small
desc: A Magnet is an object that produces
an invisible magnetic field, allowing it
to attract materials such as iron and attract
or repel other magnets. Magnets have two
poles — north and south — where the magnetic

name: Magnifying glass
desc: of an object; it consists of a large lens held in a frame with a handle. A magnifying glass
is of higher quality than a bare lens with a
minimum of 5x magnification. It can be used
to attempt to start fires using strong

name: Mirror, small metal
desc: A Mirror is useful in a dungeon
environment for many reasons. For instance,
it is the only way to look at a medusa
without being turned to stone. Mirrors are
also useful for looking around corners, and
can be used outdoors to send signals using

name: Mirror, small silver
desc: A Mirror is useful in a dungeon

without being turned to stone. Mirrors are
also useful for looking around corners, and

name: Nails, Iron (20)
desc: A Nail is a rigid metal fastener with a pointed end that is used in construction.

name: Nails, silver (20)
desc: A Nail is a rigid metal fastener with a pointed end that is used in construction. Siver Nails are specifically used for creating a decorative effect, or for sealing the coffins of undead creatures.

name: Needle, magnetized
desc: A Magnetized Needle is made by taking
an iron needle and exposing it to a magnet.
Once magnetized, the needle will act as a
weaker magnet. The magnetic properties will
usually remain as long as the needle isn't
A magnetic needle that can rotate freely will
align with magnetic North, making navigation

name: Oil (per flask)
desc: Flasks of Oil are often carried by
adventurers. A full flask has enough oil to
fill a lantern.  Flasks are also used to hurl
oil onto a surface or creature in order to

has a breaking strength of 1,600 pounds. A
safe working load for a rope is normally one-
quarter of the breaking strength. One or more
knots in a rope cut the breaking strength in
half. This does not affect the safe working
load because knots are figured into the
